Page 25, Section 6 (Concluding remarks), paragraph beginning “Limitation of the approach based on Volovikov’s theorem.”

However, it is still an open question whether or not a counterexample exists for some natural valuation functions.
title: Construct natural valuation counterexamples to envy-free multi-divisions with long knives
background_latex:
A multi-layered cake consists of \(m\) layers, each identified with the unit interval \([0,1]\). A \emph{layered piece} is an \(m\)-tuple \(L=(L_\ell)_{\ell\in[m]}\) where each \(L_\ell\subseteq[0,1]\) is a (finite union of) disjoint closed subintervals in layer \(\ell\). A \emph{multi-division} into \(q\) pieces is a \(q\)-tuple \(A=(A_1,\dots,A_q)\) of layered pieces whose interiors are pairwise disjoint and whose union covers the entire \(m\)-layered cake.

A layered piece \(L\) is \emph{contiguous} if each \(L_\ell\) is a single interval. It is \emph{non-overlapping} if for every two distinct layers \(\ell\neq \ell'\), the interiors of \(L_\ell\) and \(L_{\ell'}\) are disjoint as subsets of \([0,1]\) (equivalently, they intersect only on a finite set of points). A multi-division is \emph{feasible} if each \(A_j\) is non-overlapping, and it is \emph{contiguous} if each \(A_j\) is contiguous.

A common restricted class of feasible contiguous multi-divisions is given by \(q-1\) \emph{long knives}: one chooses cut positions \(0=t_0\le t_1\le\cdots\le t_{q-1}\le t_q=1\) and cuts \emph{every} layer at these positions, producing \(q\) intervals \([t_{r-1},t_r]\) in each layer. A feasible contiguous multi-division using \(q-1\) long knives then assigns to each bundle \(A_j\) exactly one of these \(q\) intervals from each layer, in such a way that within any fixed bundle \(A_j\), the chosen intervals from different layers have disjoint interiors in \([0,1]\).

Each agent \(i\in[n]\) has a \emph{valuation function} \(v_i\) that assigns a real value \(v_i(L)\) to every layered piece \(L\). (The paper discusses both general choice functions and valuations; here we focus on valuations as the paper’s open question does.) Envy-freeness for a multi-division \(A=(A_1,\dots,A_q)\) means there exists a surjective assignment \(\pi:[n]\to[q]\) such that every agent weakly prefers her assigned piece to any other, e.g. under valuations \(v_i(A_{\pi(i)})\ge \max_{j\in[q]} v_i(A_j)\) for all \(i\in[n]\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exist \emph{natural} valuation functions \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) (in the sense intended by the paper) for which \emph{no} envy-free multi-division exists that is both feasible and contiguous and is obtained using \(q-1\) long knives; equivalently, either:

1) construct an explicit instance (specified by \(m,n,q\) and valuations \(v_i\)) with this nonexistence property, or

2) prove that no such counterexample exists for a suitably broad and natural class of valuation functions.

The paper does not formalize what counts as “natural”; the task is to resolve existence of such counterexamples under an appropriate natural valuation-function model.

location_in_paper: Page 25, Section 6 (Concluding remarks), paragraph beginning “Limitation of the approach based on Volovikov’s theorem.”
open_problem_quote: However, it is still an open question whether or not a counterexample exists for some natural valuation functions.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can discretize valuations (e.g., piecewise-constant densities on a grid) and then brute-force over long-knife cut positions and bundle assignments to test envy-freeness, making automated search plausible. However, the space is still large and the paper does not specify what “natural” valuations mean, so search constraints may be ambiguous.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate counterexamples can be represented as parametrized piecewise-constant valuation densities across layers, with a fitness signal given by the minimum achievable envy (or infeasibility) over all long-knife divisions and assignments computed by optimization. The main challenge is the nested optimization (searching over divisions/assignments) and the ill-defined notion of “natural,” but iterative improvement could still produce informative hard instances.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper proves a strong positive result for the long-knives model under very general (choice-function) preferences: when \(q\) is a prime power and \(m\le q\le n\), there exists an envy-free feasible contiguous multi-division obtainable with \(q-1\) long knives (Theorem 1). The proof encodes long-knife feasible divisions and assignments in the chessboard complex \(\Delta_{2q-1,q}\) and applies Volovikov’s equivariant Borsuk–Ulam type theorem, together with Gale’s averaging trick and a flow/rounding lemma, to force a point where the aggregated preference vector hits the barycenter \((1/q,\dots,1/q)\). For \(m=2,q=3\) the paper also gives an existence theorem with one short and one long knife under monotone/hungry preferences and an FPTAS for \(\varepsilon\)-envy-free solutions under monotone Lipschitz valuations.

The open problem highlighted in the concluding remarks concerns the gap between the choice-function impossibility for non–prime-power \(q\) and the valuation-function setting: known counterexamples for choice functions (when \(q\) is not a prime power) do not automatically yield counterexamples for additive/continuous/Lipschitz valuations. At present, the forward-citation set provided does not contain a paper resolving this gap. Adjacent work on multi-layered cakes establishes proportional (rather than envy-free) feasible contiguous allocations under additive absolutely-continuous valuations, often using cutting primitives beyond long knives (e.g., a pair-of-knives model), suggesting that long-knife restrictions may be the true bottleneck. On the algorithmic side, reductions from approximate envy-free cake cutting with monotone preferences to approximate root-finding for monotone switching functions indicate a possible route to efficient approximation schemes if the multilayer long-knife constraints can be expressed in a suitably monotone fixed-point/root framework.

Overall, with the information available here, the problem remains open: no explicit ‘natural valuation’ counterexample to envy-free long-knife multi-divisions is identified, nor is there a theorem guaranteeing existence for broad valuation classes when \(q\) is not a prime power. Promising directions include (i) attempting to adapt non–prime-power obstruction constructions from choice functions to additive absolutely-continuous valuations while preserving feasibility/non-overlap across layers, and (ii) formulating the long-knife envy-free conditions as a continuous (possibly piecewise-linear) equivariant map problem or as a monotone root-finding instance to leverage algorithmic/topological machinery beyond Volovikov’s prime-power regime.

sources.title: Fair Division of Multi-layered Cakes
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves existence/computation results for feasible contiguous proportional (and in one case exact) multi-allocations under additive absolutely-continuous valuations: - Theorem 1: for any 2-layer cake and 2 agents, there exists an exact complete feasible multi-allocation (not necessarily contiguous). - Theorem 3: for 3 layers and 3 agents, there exists a proportional complete multi-allocation that is feasible and contiguous. - Theorem 5: for 3 layers and any number of agents \(n\ge 3\), there exists a proportional complete multi-allocation that is feasible and contiguous. - Theorems 7–8: for \(m=2^a\cdot 3\) layers (\(a\in\mathbb Z_+\)) and \(n\ge m\), there exists a proportional complete multi-allocation that is feasible and contiguous, with a recursive procedure based on majority switching points and merging diagonal pieces.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for ‘natural’ valuation counterexamples showing that envy-free feasible contiguous multi-divisions obtained using \(q-1\) long knives may fail to exist (or a proof they always exist) under a natural valuation model. This paper does not study envy-freeness under the long-knives restriction, nor does it construct impossibility instances. Instead it develops algorithms/existence proofs for proportional (and one exact) allocations using diagonal-piece cuts and a new ‘pair of knives’ moving-knife model. These techniques are adjacent to the long-knife framework (the author notes a pair of knives generalizes a long knife when one knife is at an endpoint), but they do not address whether envy-free allocations exist when all layers are cut at common \(t_1,\dots,t_{q-1}\) and bundles pick one interval per layer with non-overlap. Hence it neither resolves nor directly advances the counterexample question, except by providing alternative cutting primitives and existence results in a different fairness notion (proportionality).

sources.title: Computing approximate roots of monotone functions
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves query-complexity bounds for computing an \(\varepsilon\)-root of a Lipschitz, Miranda/positive-switching function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb R^d\) under monotonicity assumptions. In particular:
- For \(d=2\), if either a single diagonal monotonicity condition holds (e.g. \(f_1\) increasing in \(x_1\)) or a single ex-diagonal condition holds (e.g. \(f_1\) decreasing in \(x_2\)), then an \(\varepsilon\)-root can be found with \(O(\log^2(L/\varepsilon))\) value-oracle evaluations (Theorems 1.3/1.4).
- For fixed \(d\ge 2\), if all \(d^2-d\) ex-diagonal conditions hold (\(f_i\) decreasing in \(x_j\) for all \(i\ne j\)), then an \(\varepsilon\)-root can be found with \(O(\log^{\lceil(d+1)/2\rceil}(L/\varepsilon))\) evaluations (Theorem 1.6).
It also gives lower bounds showing exponential-in-digits behavior without sufficient monotonicity (Propositions 1.2, 1.5, 1.7), and a reduction from approximate group envy-free cake cutting with monotone preferences to approximate root finding (Theorem 1.9).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for natural valuation counterexamples (or impossibility) for envy-free multi-divisions of multi-layered cakes restricted to contiguous feasible allocations produced by \(q-1\) long knives. This paper does not study multi-layered cakes, long-knife feasibility/non-overlap constraints across layers, or existence/nonexistence of exact envy-free allocations under such geometric restrictions. Its cake-cutting application concerns a standard one-layer interval cake and (group) envy-freeness, and only in an approximate sense (\(r\)-near envy-free), via a reduction to finding approximate roots of certain monotone/switching functions. Thus it provides algorithmic/root-finding tools and complexity insights that might be useful if the multilayer-long-knife problem can be encoded as a Miranda-type root problem with suitable monotonicity, but it neither constructs valuation counterexamples nor proves existence for the long-knife multilayer model.
